steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: The sentence ends: "...would have cost 55 USD in fellow OECD member ______" and the blank must connect "nation" to "Costa Rica." "Costa Rica" names which nation is being discussed; it is not extra information — it is the identifying detail. That makes it a restrictive (essential) appositive, which takes no punctuation between the general noun and the specific proper noun.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — 'nation: Costa Rica.': A colon introduces a list, explanation, or elaboration. It is not the standard mark used between a common noun and a proper noun appositive that identifies it. ✗
  • Choice B — 'nation—Costa Rica.': An em dash marks a dramatic interruption or strong aside. A restrictive appositive identifying a specific member nation does not warrant an em dash and is not an aside. ✗
  • Choice C — 'nation Costa Rica.': No punctuation separates the common noun from the proper noun that restricts it. This is the standard SAT convention for a restrictive appositive — when a proper noun immediately identifies the noun it follows, no punctuation is inserted. ✓
  • Choice D — 'nation, Costa Rica.': A comma signals a non-restrictive (optional) appositive — one that adds extra but nonessential information. Here the proper noun is essential, not optional, so the comma is wrong. ✗
That matches choice C — C is correct.
